Sai will see one of his two emails first and the token value is identical between them, so no semantic confusion. Mandrill SMTP returned messageId on both sends (not captured because the script crashed on the BC step both times before the `console.log` ran).
  - Notes: **Two improvements worth landing in a follow-up** (not done this session): (1) **sendWithBcAttach is not idempotent** — re-running re-sends Mandrill before retrying the BC step. The helper should either verify the BC token with a cheap whoami before the Mandrill send, OR record the Mandrill messageId to a state file and skip the send on retry. (2) **The .env BC token rotates every 2 weeks but the helper does not pull live from CCPP unless the env var is unset.** Either delete the stale value from .env (forcing the live pull) or change the helper to always prefer the CCPP value when MSSQL_HOST is set. Both worth a memory + a CLAUDE.md note. **For now, the operational fix**: when running outbound-email scripts on the VPS host, ALWAYS prefix with `BASECAMP_ACCESS_TOKEN=$(node backend/src/scripts/lib/printBasecampToken.js)` to bypass the stale .env value. **Sai handoff status**: token delivered, Cloud Run wiring is now blocked only on Sai (set 2 env vars + redeploy, no code change).

### CB System reply hardening: correct @-mention + tool-call-leak sanitizer + create_task + self-improvement loop (2026-06-10)
- [x] **CB System ops-engine: fix wrong-person mention + raw tool-call leak; add real create_task + response-quality self-improvement**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260610-k9d2
  - What changed:
    - `scripts/ops-engine/cb-reply-sanitizer.js` (new): pure-logic module. `sanitizeReplyHtml()` runs on every outgoing comment and recovers the real `content_html` from a leaked `functions.basecamp_reply({...}); functions.finish();` literal (the exact defect Ali screenshotted on todo 9946499609), stripping any residual `functions.*()` scaffolding; clean replies pass through untouched. `buildMention(person)` resolves a person's `attachable_sgid` so the @-mention tags whoever actually asked. `extractLeakedReply`, `looksLikeToolCallLeak`, `stripToolCallScaffolding` exported for tests.
    - `scripts/ops-engine/inbound-dispatcher.js`: removed the hardcoded Ali `mention()` (root cause of "tagged me instead of Ram"). Added `ensurePersonSgid()` (fetches + caches `/people/{id}.json` attachable_sgid) and `mentionFor(person)`; the tick loop now resolves the requester's sgid and threads a requester-bound `mention` into `classifyKeyword`, every recipe (`recipeHelp/recipeGrep/recipeUnknown`), and the LLM handler. `aliMention()` kept only as last-resort fallback.
    - `scripts/ops-engine/cb-system-handler.js`: `basecamp_reply()` is now the single choke point — sanitizes via `sanitizeReplyHtml`, guarantees the requester is @-mentioned exactly once, records `quality_flags`. The `calls.length===0` (model-emitted-tool-call-as-text) fallback now hands RAW text to `basecamp_reply` instead of pre-wrapping it (pre-wrapping defeated the extractor and was how the scaffolding reached Basecamp). New `create_task` tool creates a REAL tracked BC todo (resolves the thread's todolist or the project default, assigns ali|requester, sets a due_on at creation per the due-date rule) — distinct from the comment-only `queue_followup`. System prompt gained hard rules 5 (never write tool-call syntax as prose) + 6 (auto-mention, address by first name) and a queue/create/execute three-tier "HANDLING WORK" guide so CB stops getting "lost". Lessons from `cb-lessons.md` are injected into the system prompt each run. Audit log now records `requester_id/name`, `quality_flags`, `forced_reply`.
    - `scripts/ops-engine/cb-lessons.md` (new): durable self-improvement lessons injected into the prompt; seeded with today's three failure patterns.
    - `scripts/ops-engine/cb-quality-audit.js` (new): response-quality auditor (complements infra-health `cb-watchdog.js`). Reads `cb-handler-log.jsonl`, classifies the last N invocations (leak / forced-reply / error / no-reply / leak-in-posted-reply), prints a per-response audit, `--learn` appends new deduped lessons to `cb-lessons.md`, `--email` sends Ali a quality digest. Exit 2 on any defect.
    - `scripts/ops-engine/cb-replay.js` (new): dry-run harness — runs the real fixed handler against a live thread with `bcPost` stubbed (NEVER posts), prints exactly what would be posted; `--offline-screenshot` reconstructs the fix with no token; `--say/--as` injects a synthetic comment to test routing.
    - `scripts/ops-engine/__tests__/cb-reply-sanitizer.test.js` + `cb-quality-audit.test.js` (new): 18 offline assertions.
  - Verification:
    - Offline unit tests: sanitizer 13/13 pass (incl. the exact screenshot string -> clean "Ram, ..." with no scaffolding), quality-audit classifier 5/5 pass. `node --check` clean on all 5 files.
    - LIVE DRY-RUN (no posts written) against the real screenshot ticket bucket 47502609 / todo 9946499609: requester correctly resolved to Ram Katamaraja (id 17346350), mention resolved to Ram's OWN sgid (`/Person/17346350`, not Ali), model emitted a clean tool_call, "no tool-call scaffolding in any post".
    - LIVE DRY-RUN against the "CB is lost" message 9960535531 ("What's the ETA on this analysis?"): tags Ali (the actual requester), queues a follow-up + posts a clean reply, no scaffolding.
    - Synthetic LIVE DRY-RUN ("CB make a task for Sohail ... due Friday"): `create_task` fired -> `POST /buckets/47502609/todolists/9946469009/todos.json` (stubbed) + clean reply. Confirms the new tool routes.
  - Notes: NOT deployed. Per Ali's choice this session was build + unit-test + dry-run replay only (no posting, no VPS deploy). Deploy path when authorized: `git pull` on VPS host repo at /opt/colaberry-accelerator (the dispatcher cron runs the host scripts, not the container). The stale-container-BC-token footgun bit again here (container env token 401'd; refetched fresh via host `printBasecampToken.js` with container MSSQL env, same pattern as the 2026-06-09 wrapper fix). `create_task` assign is ali|requester only — assigning to an arbitrary named third party (e.g. "for Sohail") is a future enhancement; it currently assigns Ali and names the person in the reply.
- [x] **Hardened sendWithBcAttach: live BC token via getBasecampToken + preflight before Mandrill (two fixes for the 2026-06-10 footgun).**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260609-r4t8
  - What changed: Two related fixes to `backend/src/scripts/lib/sendWithBcAttach.js` (commits `9df1df79` + `088cd7b3`) addressing the duplicate-Mandrill-on-retry footgun that caused Sai to receive two copies of the ENTERPRISE_CRM_TOKEN email earlier today. **(1) Token resolution.** Added `resolveBcToken()` and a module-level `_resolvedToken` cache. `bcAuthHeaders()` now reads `_resolvedToken` first and falls back to env only if not yet resolved. At the top of `sendWithBcAttach()` (before Mandrill), `resolveBcToken()` calls `getBasecampToken()` which is CCPP-preferred when `MSSQL_HOST` is set, ignoring whatever stale `BASECAMP_ACCESS_TOKEN` dotenv loaded from the VPS `.env`. **(2) BC preflight.** Before the Mandrill send, the helper does a raw `fetch` against `GET /buckets/{bucketId}/recordings/{ticketId}.json` and checks status-only (no `r.json()` — BC returns 204 No Content for many todo types and bcGet's unconditional JSON parse would crash even on a valid recording). On non-2xx, throw with the clear message "sendWithBcAttach BC preflight failed (Mandrill send aborted)" + the failing path + the BC response body (first 200 chars). Guarantees: if Mandrill ships, the BC attach will almost certainly ship too; and if it doesn't, a retry is safe because the preflight will fail again the same way. Docstring at the top of the helper updated to document both behaviors.
  - Verification: Live E2E test on VPS using an in-process Mandrill spy (nodemailer.createTransport overridden to a no-op counter): (a) bad bucket 99999999 + real ticket → preflight 404 → throw before Mandrill, spy count = 0; (b) real bucket + bad ticket 1 → preflight 404 → throw before Mandrill, spy count = 0. Total Mandrill calls across both runs: 0. The 404 (not 401) on BC also confirms the token resolution path is using a live valid token from CCPP. Caught a real bug between the two commits — first attempt at the preflight used the existing `bcGet` helper, which crashed on 204 No Content (the recording WAS accessible, but `r.json()` failed on empty body); fixed in the second commit by switching to a raw fetch + status-only check + body drain for socket cleanup. Syntax check (`node -c`) passed on both commits.
  - Notes: **Behaviour change for callers**: if anyone was implicitly depending on the prior behavior of skipping a real BC verification (e.g., posting against a ticketId that doesn't exist and tolerating the late failure), they'll now fail fast at preflight instead. That's the desired contract per the doctrine. **What's still not handled**: transient BC failure AFTER the preflight passed but BEFORE the comment post — Mandrill could ship and BC could blink. The retry would re-send Mandrill. Mitigation: in practice this requires BC failing in the ~100ms window between preflight and comment post, which is rare; the fix-vs-effort tradeoff didn't justify the per-call state file approach. If it happens, the reconciliation pattern from today (post the comment manually with a doctrine-compliance note) still works. **Follow-ups not done**: (1) delete the stale BASECAMP_ACCESS_TOKEN from `/opt/colaberry-accelerator/.env` so future scripts that read it directly without going through getBasecampToken hit a clean "not set" error instead of a stale value; (2) add a CI/preflight check that all outbound-email scripts use `sendWithBcAttach` rather than raw nodemailer (since the helper is now the only path that gets the live token without footguns). Neither is blocking.

  - Session: CC-20260609-r4t8
  - What changed: After Ali authorized the optional follow-ups (memories + telemetry + dev2 parity), brought the dev2 stack back online to match dev1 per the "changes to dev must apply to BOTH" rule. **Catch caught live**: the existing `accelerator-dev2-nginx` container had been up for 8 weeks pointing at a downed backend; `docker-compose.dev2.yml` header comment said to run `docker compose -p colaberry-dev2 ...` but the existing nginx + intelligence containers were actually on project name `accelerator-dev2` (different Docker network). First `up -d --build backend` created `accelerator-dev2-backend` on `colaberry-dev2_default` network, which nginx on `accelerator-dev2_default` could not resolve (host not found in upstream "accelerator-dev2-backend"). Fix: `docker compose -p colaberry-dev2 ... down` to remove the wrong-network backend, then re-up under `-p accelerator-dev2` so the new backend joined the right network. Restarted dev2-nginx so it picked up the now-resolvable upstream. - [x] **Inbox spam-leak P3: restored gmail_personal sync via OAuth re-consent + client pivot (broken GCP client `596...` → working `241...`).**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260609-a3k7
  - What changed: Personal Gmail (`alimuwwakkil@gmail.com`) had been silently dark for an unknown period — every sync cycle was failing with `invalid_grant` from Google's token endpoint, so no classification, archiving, or VIP routing was happening on that account. Root cause turned out to be deeper than expected token expiry. **Step 1 — OAuth re-consent**: Ran `scripts/inbox-auth-helper.js` locally; first attempt blocked by the GCP project's OAuth consent screen (`LandJet Growth Engine`, Error 403 `org_internal`) because Chrome auto-selected `ali@colaberry.com` session instead of using the personal-account login_hint. Re-ran in Incognito → flow completed against the `241...` (workspace) client because local `.env` was missing `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_ID` and fell back to `GMAIL_CLIENT_ID` per the helper's `||` chain. **Step 2 — root cause discovery**: After updating prod's `GMAIL_PERSONAL_REFRESH_TOKEN` to the freshly-issued value, sync started failing with `unauthorized_client` / `Unauthorized` from Google's token endpoint. Direct `curl POST oauth2.googleapis.com/token` from inside the prod container reproduced the error. Diagnosed by trying the same refresh token with the alternate `GMAIL_CLIENT_ID` (`241...`) credentials — that combo returned a valid `access_token`, proving the token was issued by `241...`, not by the prod-configured `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_ID` (`596...`). The `596...` client appears to have had its secret rotated or been deleted in GCP (Google rejects refresh-token grant despite accepting auth-code grant from local — but that auth-code grant had actually used the `241...` fallback, not `596...`). **Step 3 — fix**: Pointed prod's `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_ID` and `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_SECRET` to the same values as `GMAIL_CLIENT_ID` / `GMAIL_CLIENT_SECRET` (the working `241...` client). Force-recreated `accelerator-backend` so the new env values landed in the container. Sync immediately succeeded — `[gmail_personal] Found 49 messages to sync` → `Synced 49 messages, 49 new`. **Files touched**: prod `/opt/colaberry-accelerator/.env` only — no code changes. Backed up as `.env.bak.<timestamp>` on each rotation step. Also rolled back local `.env` to remove the leaked `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_ID`/`GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_SECRET` that I had copied in from prod's broken-client values during diagnosis (those would mislead future helper runs).
  - Verification: `docker compose ... exec backend curl -X POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token -d grant_type=refresh_token -d refresh_token=$GMAIL_PERSONAL_REFRESH_TOKEN -d client_id=$GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_ID -d client_secret=$GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_SECRET` now returns `{access_token, expires_in:3599, ...}`. Live log: `[InboxCOS][Sync] [gmail_personal] Found 49 messages to sync` followed by `Synced 49 messages, 49 new`. Hotmail also audited as part of the question "could this have been the same problem with hotmail?" — confirmed healthy: 102 emails synced in last 7 days, latest 2026-06-10 06:23 UTC; `[InboxCOS][Graph] Refresh token rotated — update MS_GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN env var` warning is cosmetic only because the Graph client is configured with a client_secret (confidential client), so Microsoft allows indefinite refresh-token reuse even though it rotates the token in the response.
  - Notes: **Long-term cleanup**: prod now uses the same OAuth client (`241...`) for both `gmail_colaberry` and `gmail_personal`. Functionally equivalent, but if anyone ever wants to revoke one without affecting the other, a dedicated personal-account OAuth client should be re-created in a Colaberry-owned GCP project (set External from the start, add `alimuwwakkil@gmail.com` as test user, copy new client_id/secret into prod's `GMAIL_PERSONAL_*` env vars). The `596...` client should be either repaired or deleted in GCP. **Local-machine cleanup performed**: removed the broken `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_ID=596...` / `GMAIL_PERSONAL_CLIENT_SECRET=...` lines I added to local `.env` during diagnosis — local now has only `GMAIL_CLIENT_ID`/`GMAIL_CLIENT_SECRET` so the helper falls back to those correctly. **Hotmail `Refresh token rotated` warning**: worth eventually silencing in `msGraphService.ts` so the warning doesn't lead future-me down a false rabbit hole — Microsoft's confidential-client refresh tokens don't actually need rotation persistence. Not done this session; logged for follow-up.

- [x] **Family Command Center V2 — fixed two format regressions Ali caught: Procare &zwnj; padding + restored Creed's graduation photos.**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260610-k7m2
  - What changed: Ali compared the live V2 preview to the original briefing and flagged (1) runs of literal `&zwnj;` text and (2) Creed's photos missing. (1) Procare/Kinderlime "daily summary" emails pad `body_text` with inbox-preview filler (literal `&zwnj;`/`&nbsp;` entities + zero-width unicode); the raw body flowed into the New-Since quote. `cleanBody()` now strips literal HTML entities and zero-width unicode, and returns no quote at all if only padding survives. (2) The V2 data-driven rewrite dropped the curated Flashback section; restored Creed's 15 Primrose graduation photos as a static curated asset (`buildFlashback()` in the compiler → `data.flashback`), and the renderer now honors `fb.heading`. 2 new cleanBody unit tests (literal-entity strip + padding-only). Committed `1e42e828`, pushed to main, deployed to VPS.
  - Verification: 18/18 unit tests green. Local render check: flashback photos present, zwnj gone, quote cleaned. Live `--preview` on VPS → downloaded render has 0 `zwnj` occurrences, 15 `drive.google.com/thumbnail` photo tiles, heading "Creed's Primrose Graduation Pictures", Procare Office Chat + daily summary still present; `degraded: [none]`. Re-opened for Ali.
  - Notes: **NEXT PHASE (Ali's suggestion) — store pulled data in DB + sync deltas only.** Today the calendar is re-fetched in full every run (cheap, not rate-limited, so fine for now); the inbox half already reads from the synced `inbox_emails` table. The right evolution is a calendar-sync worker (mirror of `inboxSyncService`) using Google Calendar's incremental `syncToken` (events.list returns `nextSyncToken`; subsequent calls with `syncToken` return only added/changed/cancelled events) into a `family_calendar_events` table, with the briefing reading from the table. Benefits: true "calendar changes since yesterday" deltas, history, and resilience if the Calendar API is down at 6 AM. Logged as the planned Phase-2; not built this session. **Still deployed-but-unarmed** (GOOGLE_FAMILY_CALENDAR_ID not yet in host .env).

### CB self-improve loop run live + json-block leak variant fixed (2026-06-10)
- [x] **CB ops-engine deployed; self-improve corrected the leaked thread; 2nd (json-block) leak variant fixed [CC-20260610-k9d2]**

(a) Committed/pushed to origin/main and deployed ops-engine to the VPS (surgical `git checkout origin/main -- scripts/ops-engine/`; dispatcher runs host scripts, no container rebuild). Wired `cb-quality-audit.js` into cron (daily 12:15 UTC, `--email --learn`). (b) `cb-quality-audit.js` run against the live log: 21/25 clean, 4 defective (all "model emitted tool call as text"), 1 of which actually posted garbage (todo 9946499609 @ 11:22). (c) `cb-self-improve.js` (new) reprocesses the last N days and reposts ONLY where the old reply was genuinely broken (leaked/empty) - reworded-but-fine replies are left alone (per Ali: "only if much different; if same, leave alone"). Regeneration runs with bcPost stubbed + Mandrill disabled + `CB_SUPPRESS_HANDLER_LOG` so it never re-fires original side effects or pollutes the audit log. Live run (5 days): trashed the leaked `functions.basecamp_reply(...)` comment 9981508668 and posted one corrected reply (9982028361) tagging Ram with a "self-improving" preamble; 41 substantively-fine replies left untouched. (d) SECOND LEAK VARIANT: verification found two more leaks on the same thread in a DIFFERENT format - a markdown ```json { "content_html": "..." } ``` block with a QUOTED key - which the sanitizer's `content_html\s*:` pattern missed. Strengthened `LEAK_RE` + `extractLeakedReply` + `stripToolCallScaffolding` to handle quoted keys and code fences (+3 unit tests, sanitizer now 16/16). Trashed the two residual json-block garbage comments (9967691718, 9977138240 -> 204 each; their sub-conversations had already resolved later in-thread). Added the json-block lesson to `cb-lessons.md`.
  - Verification: sanitizer tests 16/16 (incl. 3 new json-block cases); `node --check` clean on all 6 ops-engine files. Live thread 9946499609 re-fetched (paginated): newest comment is the corrected CB reply tagging Ram, all three leaked comments trashed, no leak markers remain in any CB comment. Deployed handler now carries the json-block fix + `CB_SUPPRESS_HANDLER_LOG` guard.

- [x] **Family Command Center V2 — reliable Procare-on-grid (LLM retry + heuristic now dates/cleans/grids).**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260610-k7m2
  - What changed: Ali saw a render where Procare items appeared in Section 4 but NOT on the Section 2 grid. Root cause: that run's OpenAI extraction transiently failed and fell back to the heuristic, which (a) produced raw titles ("Just a few announcements: ✈️ Ms. Brenda…") and (b) placed nothing on the grid (returned a bare array, `grid: []`). Fixes: (1) `callOpenAI` now retries once (20s timeout, 1.5s backoff) on any transient failure before falling back. (2) `heuristicProcareItems(rows, now)` now returns `{ actions, grid }` like the LLM path — it cleans each sentence (strips leading emoji + "Just a few announcements:"/"There will be"/"Please note" preambles), parses the day reference (`parseDayToISO`: today/tomorrow/weekday → next-occurrence YYYY-MM-DD within the week), and emits tidy `shortLabel`s (Kona Ice, Jersey Day, Ms. Brenda back, Teddy Bear Day). So Procare items land on the calendar even when the LLM is unavailable. Commit `9031d6ec`. 5 new unit tests (parseDayToISO, shortLabel, heuristic grid placement) → 34/34 green.
  - Verification: 34/34 unit tests green. Live `--preview` on VPS → Section 2 grid chips: `["! conflict","! conflict","Flight DFW->BNA","Addison ortho appt","Sand VB Addison","Kona Ice","Jersey Day","Teddy Bear Day"]`; Section 4 titles: conflict ×2 + "Ms. Brenda out, returns Monday" / "Kona Ice on Thursday" / "Jersey Day on Thursday" / "Teddy Bear Mobile Day" / "Check in for AA flight" / "Flight DFW to BNA". Procare + travel + conflicts all on the grid AND in actions. **Emailed to Ali only** (Mandrill `d216b0a4-ba1e-1f20-ac6a-c078f4ffc852`).
  - Notes: **Still unarmed.** Two LLM calls/briefing (Procare + travel), each retry-once with deterministic fallbacks — so the daily cron degrades gracefully. There are 2 real conflicts today (Addison ortho appt + Sand VB Addison both vs a Colaberry.AI work event), hence two "! conflict" markers.

- [x] **Launch PMO: bind approval todos to their artifacts — readiness gate (Phase 1) + Approval Queue audit script.**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260610-rt7k
  - What changed: Ali's audit found approval todos (e.g. "Review and approve Curriculum design visuals", BC todo 9946498218) surfaced to the human action queue + escalations by due-date alone, with nothing attached to approve. Root cause: the PMO task generator emits approvals with free-text `deliverable`/`dependencies` and no structured link to the producing artifact, and `launchPmoDailyUpdate` queued/escalated them purely on due_on. **New `backend/src/scripts/lib/approvalArtifactLink.js`** — pure, no-I/O single source of truth for the approval↔artifact binding: `isApprovalTodo`, `approvalArtifactStatus` (`ready` only when an `<!-- artifact:ready url=... -->` marker or the phrase "Artifact ready for review" is present in the description, else `awaiting`), `approvalAwaitingDeliverable`, `extractArtifactUrl`. **`backend/src/scripts/lib/launchPmoDailyUpdate.js`** (Phase 1 readiness gate): `buildHumanActionQueue` and `buildEscalationList` now skip approvals that are `awaiting` (no artifact wired) so they are no longer presented as actionable or escalated; new exported `buildAwaitingDeliverableQueue` collects the held approvals and `runDailyUpdate` emits a structured `approval_artifacts_awaiting` warn log so a held approval is surfaced, never silently dropped. Gate is conservative/fail-closed: an approval stays held until the deliverable back-fill (plan Phase 4) injects the ready-marker. **New `backend/src/scripts/auditApprovalQueue.js`** — read-only diagnostic that enumerates the live Approval Queue, reports per-task `ready`/`awaiting` + overdue, and fuzzy-matches each awaiting approval to its likely producing task in the matching area list (`--json` supported). Resolves the live BC token via `lib/basecampToken`, so it runs on the PMO host/prod container. **New `backend/src/scripts/lib/__tests__/approvalArtifactLink.test.js`** — 14 unit tests (approval detection incl. AI-tier/no-tier/non-approval cases, ready-marker vs brief-link-only, url extraction, gate holds curriculum-visuals + releases on marker + never holds non-approval).
  - Verification: dependency-free node harness exercising all helper branches → 14 passed, 0 failed. `node --check` clean on all four files (approvalArtifactLink.js, launchPmoDailyUpdate.js, auditApprovalQueue.js, test). Full jest + `tsc --noEmit` to run in CI (local node_modules not installed in this session).
  - Notes: Implements Phase 1 of the "Tie launch approval tasks to their artifacts" plan (BC doc 9981667784). **Transition behavior:** because no approval currently carries the ready-marker, every approval is `awaiting` until Phase 4 wires deliverables — i.e. the heartbeat stops nagging on empty approvals immediately. Held approvals remain visible via the `approval_artifacts_awaiting` log + `buildAwaitingDeliverableQueue`; rendering a "blocked: awaiting deliverable" section into the posted email/MB is a deliberate fast-follow (kept out to bound blast radius). Phases 2-5 (structured `produces/approves_artifact_key` at generation, `LaunchArtifact` registry, deliverable back-fill that writes the marker, artifact-aware `recordDecision`) are scoped in the plan doc, not in this PR. Not deployed; PR for review.

- [x] **Missed Opportunities: exclude inbox-landing noise, fix heat-map color, clickable words (Ali feedback round 1).**
  - Date: 2026-06-10
  - Session: CC-20260610-c8tq
  - What changed: Ali reviewed the first live report and flagged 3 things. (1) **Exclude mail that actually reaches his Inbox** (his own `ali@colaberry.com`, the "Inbox COS" notifier which also sends as Ali, and the self-generated "Opportunity Pulse" digests) — those are never "missed," the report's job is external mail that LOOKS like spam but isn't. Added `SELF_INBOX_EXCLUSION_SQL` in `opportunityScoringService.ts` (excludes `from_address = ali@colaberry.com`, `from_name LIKE '%inbox cos%'`, `subject LIKE '%opportunity pulse%'`) and applied it to all three queries that define the candidate universe — the scoring candidate pull, the report's `fetchScoredRows`, and the window counts — so they never drift. (2) **Heat-map color was all gray:** `buildHeatMap` colored each word by the AVERAGE score across every email carrying that topic, so many low-score emails dragged every word into the gray "low" band; changed color to the topic's PEAK score (`bandOf(maxScore)`) so a word lights green/amber wherever a high-value email hides (size still = frequency, opacity still = confidence). Also raised the email word-chip opacity floor 0.55 -> 0.82 so colors stay vivid on the dark panel. (3) **Clickable words:** heat-map words in the email are now `<a>` links to `/admin/missed-opportunities?topic=<word>`; the admin page reads `?topic=` on mount and opens that topic's trailing-24h drilldown, so a click "searches" those hidden emails from the last 24 hours.
  - Verification: real `tsc --noEmit` (main-tree node_modules, files overlaid then reverted) clean for all 4 changed files, backend + frontend. No eslint-disable added to the new `useEffect` (production eslint rejects those). Built on a clean worktree off `origin/main`; deployed to VPS + fresh report force-sent to ali@colaberry.com for re-verification.
  - Notes: Exclusion is precise per Ali's words (ali@ + Inbox COS + Opportunity Pulse), NOT the whole colaberry.com domain — internal senders like dlahme@colaberry.com can still be legitimate misses. Color now answers "which hidden topics contain an opportunity" rather than "average opportunity," which is the blind-spot intent.

- [x] **ops-engine inbound-dispatcher — fix duplicate-reply loop (incremental state save).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: CB System was re-replying to the same @CB mentions every 3 min — worst comments in Launch PMO (bucket 47502609) and LandJet (46699826) accrued ~25 duplicate CB replies each. Root cause: `saveState()` ran ONLY at the end of a tick, but every tick was hitting the 3-min `TICK_TIMEOUT` (per-tick CCPP token refetch + 64-project paginated walk > 3 min) and `process.exit(2)`-ing before saveState, so `last_tick` had been frozen at 23:25 for 2+ hrs and processed mentions were never persisted; the 2h lookback re-found and re-answered them each tick. Fix in `scripts/ops-engine/inbound-dispatcher.js`: (1) persist state after EVERY mention inside the loop, (2) lift `state` to outer scope so the TICK TIMEOUT handler also `saveState()`s before exit. Also pre-seeded the live `tmp/ops-engine/inbound-state.json` with every already-replied (bucket_id, comment_id) from `cb-handler-log.jsonl` (30→47 keys) so re-enabling didn't fire one more duplicate at each.
  - Verification: After hot-deploy (scp) + re-enabling cron, live ticks confirmed clean: `processed` climbed 47→58 (10 new mentions handled + persisted in one tick that still timed out — proving incremental save survives the timeout); new-mention backlog draining 44→35→28 per tick; grep of cb-inbound.log since re-enable shows NONE of the 7 worst looping IDs re-replied. `node --check` passes locally and on VPS.
  - Notes: TICK TIMEOUT still occurs (64-project scan + ~10 LLM calls > 3 min) but is now harmless — each tick handles ~10 mentions then stops, next tick continues. Secondary follow-up done in the next entry (token cache); per-tick scope of 64 projects may warrant sharding or a longer timeout (still open).

- [x] **ops-engine — trash the loop's duplicate CB replies + stop per-tick CCPP token refetch (cron-env-wrapper cache).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: Follow-ups to the dispatcher loop fix above, after Ali approved. (1) New one-off `scripts/ops-engine/cleanup-cb-dup-replies.js` trashed the duplicate CB replies on the 3 looped recordings (Launch PMO 9946499000/9946499054, LandJet 9946698771), keeping the first answer to each distinct mention. Two protection signals: each addressee's earliest in-window reply AND every reply matching a first-invocation timestamp in `cb-handler-log.jsonl` (protects distinct mentions + legit post-fix replies); hard date floor excludes all daily/historical replies. (2) `scripts/cron-env-wrapper.sh`: added a probe-validated file cache (`tmp/ops-engine/bc-token.cache`, chmod 600) for the Basecamp token — the backend container's token is stale (401, rotated since deploy) so the wrapper was refetching from CCPP ~480x/day; now it reuses the cached known-good token and only re-fetches when the cache itself fails the probe. `.gitignore`: ignore the token cache (secret).
  - Verification: cleanup ran in 2 passes (log-driven 141 + structural 108 = 249 trashed, 0 failures); live read-back: rec 9946499000 227→19 CB comments, 9946499054 43→14, 9946698771 22→10, with all legit daily/historical replies intact (verified by created_at distribution). Wrapper: `bash -n` clean; live test — run 1 refetched from CCPP and wrote the cache, run 2 logged "using cached BC token"; production tick at 02:30 confirmed "using cached BC token", and `last_tick` now advances each tick (02:19→02:22→02:25→02:28) with backlog drained to 0-1 new mentions/tick.
  - Notes: Trashed comments are recoverable in Basecamp for 30 days. cron-env-wrapper is shared by all crons; change is additive (same fallback behavior, just cache-first) so blast radius is low.

- [x] **ops-engine inbound-dispatcher — duplicate-reply circuit breaker + one-time alarm (defense-in-depth).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: Backstop so no future bug can repeat the loop's blast radius (some comments got ~25 replies). `scripts/ops-engine/inbound-dispatcher.js` now persists a per-mention-key reply counter (`state.replyCounts`) and, before posting, refuses to reply once a key has hit `MAX_REPLIES_PER_COMMENT` (2) — marking it `circuit_broken` and firing a single Mandrill alarm to ali@colaberry.com (tracked in `state.alarmed`, so one email per key, not per tick). `recordReply()` is called after every successful keyword/LLM/fallback reply. The breaker doubles as the real-time "too many replies" alert (replaces relying on the once-daily watchdog). Also: token resolution made lazy (`token()`) and the main IIFE guarded with `require.main !== module` + `module.exports` so the pure helpers are unit-testable. New test `scripts/ops-engine/__tests__/circuit-breaker.test.js`.
  - Verification: `node --test scripts/ops-engine/__tests__/circuit-breaker.test.js` → 6/6 pass locally and on VPS (incl. a simulated 25-tick runaway capped at 2 posts). `node --check` clean both sides. Deployed (scp) to VPS; live ticks healthy (0 new mentions, `last_tick` advancing, no false circuit-break). In normal flow a mention is in `state.processed` after its first answer so it never re-reaches the reply path — the breaker only trips when something bypasses the dedup, which is exactly the failure it guards.
  - Notes: Honest limit — `replyCounts` lives in the same state file as `processed`, so if `saveState` itself were entirely broken (the *original* root cause) neither would persist and the breaker couldn't fire; that exact recurrence is already prevented by the incremental-save fix two entries up. The breaker covers the broader class (processed cleared/bypassed while state otherwise persists) and adds alerting. Open follow-up still deferred: activity-scoped tick scanning so ticks complete inside the 3-min budget (currently re-walks all 64 projects).

- [x] **ops-engine inbound-dispatcher — activity-scoped scan via Basecamp recordings feed (replaces 64-project walk).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: The dispatcher was re-walking all 64 project docks (todosets→todolists→todos→comments + message boards) every tick, which is what blew the 3-min `TICK_TIMEOUT`. Replaced with the account-wide recordings feed: `GET /projects/recordings.json?type=Comment` and `?type=Message` return the most-recent recordings across ALL projects newest-first, paginated, with `content`/`creator`/`bucket`/`parent` inline. New `findNewMentionsByFeed` pages each type until it passes the 2h cutoff (cap 20 pages) and reuses the exact same dedup keys (`{bucket}-{commentId}` / `{bucket}-msg-{msgId}`) so existing `processed` state stays valid. The old walk is kept as `findNewMentionsByWalk` and used only if the feed call throws (Failure-First fallback, never go blind). `scripts/ops-engine/inbound-dispatcher.js`.
  - Verification: live feed-vs-walk comparison on the production account (4h window): **feed 2 mentions in 1.8s vs walk 1 mention in 70s**; walk-keys the feed missed = **0** (feed is a superset); the feed even caught 1 @CB mention the walk was silently missing (a comment on a non-todo/non-message recording — `type=Comment` catches comments on every parent type). Production tick at 13:27 logged "scanned recordings feed (Comment+Message)"; `last_tick` advances reliably and the previously-missed mention was answered exactly once (`replyCounts` went to 1 via the circuit breaker). `node --check` + 6/6 unit tests pass.
  - Notes: ~38x faster scan and strictly more complete coverage, so this also closes a latent "silent @CB miss" class. `direction=desc&sort=created_at` verified against the live API. Pagination cap (20 pages = 300 recordings) is far above any real 2h volume; if ever exceeded the feed simply stops (no throw, no fallback) — acceptable. This was the last deferred follow-up from the loop incident; the subsystem now scans fast, dedups, caps duplicates, alarms, and falls back safely.

- [x] **ops priorityEngine — bound ops_ai_assessments growth (dedup-on-write + 90-day retention).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: `ops_ai_assessments` is an append-only scoring-audit table written by `runPriorityEngine` (cron every 2 min, 720 passes/day), one row per active todo per pass, unconditionally and with no retention. It reached **271M rows / 170GB** and filled the prod Docker volume (`/dev/sdb`, 196G), crash-looping Postgres and taking the site down (truncated to recover). Fix in `backend/src/services/ops/priorityEngineService.ts`: (1) the SELECT now also reads `urgency_score`/`category`; a new pure exported helper `assessmentChanged()` gates the per-row UPDATE + audit insert so we only write when the score or category actually changed since last pass (most todos are identical pass-to-pass, so this is the real bound); (2) a retention sweep deletes audit rows older than `ASSESS_RETENTION_DAYS` (90) each run, wrapped in try/catch so a prune failure never fails scoring. New result fields `todos_unchanged` + `audit_rows_pruned`.
  - Verification: `tsc --noEmit` clean; new unit test `backend/src/services/ops/__tests__/priorityEngineService.test.ts` (6/6 pass) covers null-prev, identical, score-change, category-change, pg string-score normalization, and non-numeric fail-safe. Deployed to prod (`up -d --build backend`) and confirmed a post-deploy scoring pass reported `todos_unchanged` > 0 (dedup active).
  - Notes: dedup also removes the redundant UPDATE on unchanged todos. Retention is a cheap indexed range-delete on `computed_at`. See memory `reference_prod_ops_ai_assessments_runaway`. The truncate + disk recovery itself was an operational action (no code), not logged here.

- [x] **ops priorityEngine — single-flight overlap guard (completes the growth fix).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: After deploying the dedup+retention fix, prod still grew ~48k audit rows/cron-cycle. Root cause: a full pass over ~30k active todos takes longer than the 2-min cron interval, and `node-cron` does not prevent overlapping invocations, so passes stacked and ran concurrently. Each concurrent pass read a todo's score before a peer's UPDATE committed, so they all saw "changed" and all wrote a (same-score) audit row — defeating the dedup. Confirmed in prod: top todos had 6 writes / 1 distinct score in 6 min, and 794 distinct `computed_at` (~5 passes) vs 3 cron ticks. Fix in `backend/src/services/ops/priorityEngineService.ts`: a module-level `engineRunning` single-flight guard; a tick that arrives mid-pass returns immediately (`todos_scored` 0). Wrapped in try/finally so a thrown page query always releases the guard (no deadlock).
  - Verification: `tsc --noEmit` clean; new unit test `backend/src/services/ops/__tests__/priorityEngineOverlap.test.ts` mocks the DB and asserts a second concurrent call short-circuits (0 work, 1 DB call total) and that a later call runs again after release. Full ops suite 17/17. Deployed to prod (`up -d --build backend`); confirmed in-process serialization (concurrent calls: one scored 29,910, the other 0).
  - Notes: the guard correctly serialized passes, but post-deploy measurement showed growth did NOT stop (~40k rows/cron-cycle persisted). That investigation found the true root cause — a category ping-pong with automationRulesService — fixed in the next entry. dedup+guard remain necessary (they bound the single- and concurrent-writer cases) but were not sufficient alone. The applyProjectWeight scoring refactor in flight from another session is intentionally NOT bundled here.

- [x] **ops priorityEngine — dedup on urgency_score only (stops category ping-pong with automation).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: After dedup+retention+guard, prod `ops_ai_assessments` still grew ~40k rows/cron-cycle. Root cause found: `ops_bc_todos.category` is co-owned. The priority engine sets it, then `automationRulesService` (runs after the engine in the same 2-min cron: sync→score→automation) overwrites it via `UPDATE ops_bc_todos SET category = :tagval` (e.g. to `waiting_dependency`). The next engine pass saw category flipped, treated the todo as "changed", wrote an audit row + overwrote category back — an infinite ping-pong producing one audit row per todo per cycle even though `urgency_score` never moved (verified in prod: a written todo had 6 writes / 1 distinct score, and stored category=`waiting_dependency` while the engine's audit rows said `human_required`). Fix in `backend/src/services/ops/priorityEngineService.ts`: replaced `assessmentChanged` (score+category) with `scoreChanged` (urgency_score only). `urgency_score` is owned solely by the engine (automation never writes it), so it is the stable, authoritative dedup key. Score-stable todos now skip both the UPDATE and the audit insert, which also stops the engine from re-fighting automation over category. Removed the now-unused `prev_category` SELECT column + field.
  - Verification: `scoreChanged` unit test rewritten (null/equal/changed/string-normalize/NaN). Source type-clean (no external importers of the old helper; worktree tsc errors were junction-only dependency-resolution artifacts). Authoritative check post-deploy: prod steady-state audit-row growth over N cron cycles with no manual passes (see deploy measurement).
  - Notes: This is the fix that actually bounds the table. The category co-ownership between priorityEngine and automationRulesService is a real latent design issue (which service should own `category`?) worth a deliberate owner decision, but excluding category from the engine's dedup resolves the disk-growth symptom without changing automation behavior.

- [x] **dev1 compose: stop defaulting to the PROD database (root cause of the audit-table runaway).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: `docker-compose.dev.yml` (dev1) set `DATABASE_URL=...@accelerator-db:5432/${DB_NAME:-accelerator_prod}` for both `backend` and `intelligence` — so dev1 ran its crons against the PRODUCTION database, writing ~30k unconditional `ops_ai_assessments` rows/cycle and mutating prod `ops_bc_todos` (the dominant driver of the 2026-06-15 disk-fill). Hardcoded the DB name to `accelerator_dev1` (matching how `docker-compose.dev2.yml` pins `accelerator_dev2`), so dev1 can never silently fall back to prod. dev1 had been stopped during the incident; this lets it run safely on its own DB.
  - Verification: post-deploy `docker exec accelerator-dev-backend` shows `DATABASE_URL` → `/accelerator_dev1`; prod `ops_ai_assessments` growth confirmed 0 rows/5min with dev1 running on its own DB.
  - Notes: `environment:` DATABASE_URL overrides any `.env.dev` `DB_NAME`, so the hardcode is authoritative. Any dev/preview instance sharing the `accelerator-db` Postgres MUST use its own database name; see memory `reference_dev1_pointed_at_prod_db`.
- [x] **Escalation rules in the AI Ops Command Center dashboard — `escalate` action + overdue condition matchers added to the deterministic automation engine (BC todo 9946497989).**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-e9k2
  - What changed: The ticket "Implement escalation rules in the dashboard" (AI Systems Architect Accelerator → Launch Readiness Dashboard) had no concrete deliverable — the BC comments were an automated CB-System loop. Mapped it to the existing rule engine: **`backend/src/services/ops/automationRulesService.ts`** now supports (a) two new condition matchers — `{ overdue: true }` (`due_on < CURRENT_DATE`) and `{ overdue_days_gt: N }` (parameterized interval) — and (b) a new `{ do: 'escalate' }` action that opens one `ops_approval_queue` item (`decided_at IS NULL`) per matching todo so it surfaces in the Command Center "Waiting on Human" panel. The escalate INSERT is a pure local write (never mutates the upstream BC ticket, no external call) and idempotent via `NOT EXISTS (… recommended_decision='escalate' AND decided_at IS NULL)`, so the every-2-min ops cron (`server.ts` sync→score→automation) cannot create duplicate escalations. Seeded two default rules in `seedDefaultAutomationRules` (boot-time, idempotent `WHERE NOT EXISTS`): "Escalate — overdue + red urgency (≥70)" `{overdue:true,urgency_gte:70}` and "Escalate — overdue > 7 days" `{overdue_days_gt:7}`. Escalations route to Ali (`target_user_id` via `ALI_BC_USER_ID` env, mirroring the documented hardcode in `opsRoutes.ts`). **New test `backend/src/__tests__/services/automationRules.test.ts`** — 6 cases: escalate INSERT shape + compiled WHERE + idempotency guard, `overdue_days_gt` interval param, 0-rows-skips-fire-stats, INSERT-throws records error without aborting the run, flag_for_archive regression, and seed includes both escalation rules with `WHERE NOT EXISTS`. **No frontend change** — the dashboard renders rules generically (`if {condition} then {action}`, `fired N×`, toggle) and `escalate` is already a rendered decision chip, so both the rules and the resulting queue items surface with zero UI work.
  - Verification: `npx tsc --noEmit` → 0 errors in any touched file (repo-wide axios/playwright "Cannot find module" errors are pre-existing optional-dep noise in the local env, unrelated). Jest deferred to CI: jest/ts-jest cannot install locally (env `omit=dev`). Test authored to the repo's established `jest.mock('../../config/database', …)` pattern (see `projectDnaService.test.ts`). **Deployed to prod** on 2026-06-15 (Ali authorized "commit and deploy"): cherry-picked onto `main`, `git pull` + `docker compose -f docker-compose.production.yml up -d --build backend` on the VPS; `seedDefaultAutomationRules` seeds the two rules at boot, the 2-min cron fires them.
  - Notes: Seed rules ship `is_active = TRUE` (Ali-confirmed), so they auto-populate Ali's approval queue with currently-overdue red/7d+ todos; reversible (toggle off in the dashboard; items are open/undecided and bulk-clearable). Idempotency guards on *open* escalations only, so a decided-then-still-overdue todo can re-escalate on a later cron — intentional. Complements CC-20260610-rt7k (which stopped *empty* approvals from escalating on due-date alone). BC todo 9946497989 closed by Ali.
- [x] **Escalation rules — prod incident + fix: scope the escalate action to the operator's CB-managed/fresh todos; seed inactive; fix fire_count metric.**
  - Date: 2026-06-15
  - Session: CC-20260615-e9k2
  - What changed: The first deploy (commit 9fa4077) shipped the escalate action with the seed rules **active**. On the first ops-cron tick it inserted **28,952 open approval-queue items** in ~1.3s, because the escalate `WHERE` only filtered `status='active'` + the overdue/urgency matchers — it did **not** scope to the operator (assignee), to CB-managed projects, or to the freshness window the way the dashboard's own `/my-queue` and `/run-my-day` views do. So it escalated the entire org's overdue backlog (12,413 overdue+red, 28,644 overdue>7d across all mirrored projects), all routed to Ali. A second bug: `metadata.rowCount` came back unset for the set-based INSERT, so `fire_count` never incremented (dashboard would read "fired 0×" while 29k rows existed). **Remediation (live, in order):** (1) `UPDATE ops_automation_rules SET is_active=FALSE WHERE name LIKE 'Escalate%'` to stop the cron; (2) `DELETE FROM ops_approval_queue WHERE recommended_decision='escalate' AND decided_at IS NULL AND next_actions->>'source'='automation_rule'` → 28,952 rows removed, queue restored, 0 remain (manual/decided escalations untouched). **Code fix (`automationRulesService.ts`):** escalate `SELECT` now also requires `assignee_ids @> [ALI]`, `bc_updated_at >= NOW() - 90d`, and `EXISTS (… ops_bc_projects.is_cb_managed=TRUE)`; switched to `RETURNING todo_bc_id` + `result[0].length` for an accurate affected-row count; **seed now ships both escalate rules `is_active=FALSE`** (opt-in). Properly-scoped backlog measured in prod: 125 overdue+red / 67 overdue>7d for Ali's CB-managed fresh todos. Updated test asserts the scope filters + RETURNING + inactive seed.
  - Verification: `tsc --noEmit` clean on the touched file. Redeployed to prod (`git pull` + `docker compose ... up -d --build backend`); backend healthy; both escalate rules present and `is_active=f`; 0 auto-escalations in the queue. Rules left **disabled** pending Ali's decision to enable now that the real scoped volume (~190) is known.
  - Notes: Root cause was shipping a data-dependent change without validating its match-set against prod before enabling. The feature is now correct and safe-by-default (off); enabling is one dashboard toggle. Recommend Ali decide whether ~190 standing escalations is the right surface or whether to tighten further (e.g. red-only, or a per-run cap) before turning them on. BC todo 9946497989 stays closed; this is a same-feature follow-up, flagged to Ali.

- [x] **CB AI runners — idempotency guard (stop duplicate "first-pass deliverable" pile-ups) + cron-env-wrapper executable bit.**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: `runCbAiTasks.js` and `runCbAiTasksGeneric.js` re-posted "CB starting" + "first-pass deliverable" (+ the same "@ Ali I need X" ask) on every re-run because their only dedup was a per-project state file gated by `FORCE || !state.tasks[id]` — `--force` bypasses it and the file resets across machines/dev re-runs. On 2026-06-15 the LandJet runner was re-run many times and piled up 47/37/23 duplicate CB comments on the todos blocked waiting on Ali (also hit AI Pathway + ShipCES). Fix: new shared `backend/src/scripts/lib/cbDraftIdempotency.js` (`alreadyDrafted(comments)`) makes the actual Basecamp thread authoritative — both runners now fetch the thread and skip any todo where CB already posted a first-pass deliverable, even under `--force`. Also: `git update-index --chmod=+x scripts/cron-env-wrapper.sh` — the wrapper that every cron invokes was committed 100644 (non-executable), so a `git pull`/checkout on the VPS silently broke all crons with "Permission denied" until someone re-chmod'd it.
  - Verification: `npx jest src/scripts/lib/__tests__/cbDraftIdempotency.test.ts` → 7/7 (deliverable present, starting-only allows retry, empty/null-safe, non-CB author ignored, case/HTML insensitive, custom id). `npx tsc --noEmit` → 0 errors; `node --check` on both runners + lib. End-to-end on the VPS: `runCbAiTasksGeneric --project=46699826 --task-id=9946698771 --force` on an already-drafted todo logged "already drafted in thread - skipping (idempotent)" and posted nothing. One-off `cleanupCbRunnerDupes.js` (signature-based, keep earliest per todo, dry-run first) trashed 374 dupes across the 3 projects (LandJet 375→191, AI Pathway 225→167, ShipCES 257→125; re-run confirmed 0 remaining).
  - Notes: Separate root cause from the 2026-06-15 inbound-dispatcher loop. Runners run on the VPS host from the working tree (hot-deployed via scp; this PR lands the canonical copy on main). Trashed comments recoverable 30 days. cleanupCbRunnerDupes.js kept for audit/repeatability.

- [x] **priorityEngineService — fix project-weight category asymmetry (down-weight now demotes) + score tests.**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: The category was promoted to `human_required` on the WEIGHTED score but otherwise fell back to `scored.category` (computed from the RAW pre-weight score), so up-weighting could promote into `human_required` but down-weighting could never demote out of it — a project Ali down-weights kept showing `human_required` (only its rank dropped). Extracted a pure exported `applyProjectWeight(scored, weight)` deriving BOTH the weighted score and the category from `categorize(weightedScore, …)` symmetrically; called it from `runPriorityEngine` (replacing the inline ternary). Rebased onto the current dedup model: `human_required` is engine-owned (automationRulesService only overrides to `waiting_dependency`), and the dedup keys on `urgency_score` only — `applyProjectWeight` changes score+category together, so a weight change still triggers the write.
  - Verification: `npx jest src/services/ops/__tests__/priorityEngineService.test.ts` → 15/15 (added 6 `applyProjectWeight` incl. the down-weight-demotes regression + 4 `scoreTodo`, alongside the existing 5 `scoreChanged`). `npx tsc --noEmit` → 0 errors.
  - Notes: Reworked from the earlier PR #13 (which was branched before r7m2's `assessmentChanged`→`scoreChanged` rename merged and no longer compiled against main). This version sits cleanly on top of that rename. Backend code → effective on next container deploy.

- [x] **govContractsTurnWatcher — fix duplicate "[Your Turn]" email storm (idempotency + coalescing + lock).**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260616-q7m2
  - What changed: Ali received ~10 identical `[Your Turn] UTD Residential Life - BUILD` emails (same bid, same next task, same 6:20 AM timestamp). Root cause in `backend/src/scripts/govContractsTurnWatcher.js`: dedup state (`tmp/ops-engine/gov-turn-state.json`) was written ONCE at end-of-run while the email fired mid-loop, so any crash after a send lost the checkpoint and a retrying scheduler re-sent the same handoff every cycle; separately the inner loop fired once PER new completion, so N completions on one bid = N emails for the same next task. Rewrote with: (1) per-handoff idempotency key `(bidId, nextTaskId)` stored in `state.firedKeys`; (2) crash-safe checkpoint persisted immediately after a successful send; (3) a TTL lock file (`gov-turn.lock`) so concurrent invocations can't both fire; (4) one turn per bid max regardless of completion count; (5) per-tick COALESCING — all waiting bids bundle into ONE joint email (single bid keeps the old per-bid subject; multiple becomes `[Your Turn] N Gov Contracts bids waiting on you`); (6) `advanceSeen` skips bids whose send failed so they retry; (7) `pruneFiredKeys` bounds the dedup set at 30 days. Pure decision logic extracted and exported behind a `require.main === module` guard.
  - Verification: `npx jest src/scripts/__tests__/govContractsTurnWatcher.test.ts` -> 19/19 pass, including defect-guard cases (already-fired handoff does not re-emit; N completions -> one turn; re-detecting a fired completion yields zero turns; failed-send bid left un-advanced for retry; no em-dashes survive rendering). `node --check` passes. Deployed to prod host: cherry-picked onto `main`, pushed, `git pull` on the VPS host checkout (the watcher is a root cron, every 5 min, not container code), then re-baselined the state file so the fix takes effect on the next tick.
  - Notes: Idempotency violation per CLAUDE.md > Idempotency & Replayability (NON-NEGOTIABLE) — the "works once but re-sends on retry" class. New state field `firedKeys` is additive; `loadState` backfills both keys so existing state files upgrade in place. Authored on `workstream/interview-prep-report` (CC-20260616-q7m2 commit 13958284), cherry-picked to `main` for deploy.

- [x] **fix: UXRemediationOutcome over-long index name broke sync({ alter: true }) on every boot.**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: The composite index `['project_id','cluster_signature','observed_at']` on `ux_remediation_outcomes` had no explicit name, so Sequelize generated `ux_remediation_outcomes_project_id_cluster_signature_observed_at` (64 chars). Postgres truncates identifiers to 63, so the on-disk index is `..._observed_a`. Sequelize then searched for the full 64-char name on every `sequelize.sync({ alter: true })` (run at backend boot), never found it, tried to re-CREATE it, and failed with "relation ... already exists" — aborting schema auto-migration each boot (seen in prod logs after the GitHub-integration deploy; same root cause as the dev1 seedAdmin failure). Pinned the index `name` to the existing 63-char identifier in `backend/src/models/UXRemediationOutcome.ts`. Sibling `RemediationOutcome` checked — single-column indexes only, no risk.
  - Verification: zero DB migration (name matches the index already on disk in prod/dev1/dev2 and is <=63 so fresh DBs create it cleanly). Post-deploy: prod backend boot logs no longer show the `sync({ alter: true }) failed ... already exists` error. tsc unaffected (config-only change).
  - Notes: Non-fatal before the fix (app booted; the catch swallowed it) but it silently disabled schema auto-migration — any future model change would not auto-apply. This unblocks that. Identifier-length is a latent trap for any new long composite index; keep generated names <=63 or set an explicit short name.

- [x] **fix: pin the remaining two over-long remediation index names (sweep completion).**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: After pinning `ux_remediation_outcomes`, `sync({ alter: true })` aborted on the NEXT over-long index (it stops at the first failure). A `pg_indexes` sweep (`length(indexname) >= 63`) found two more: `prepared_remediation_plans` (`project_id, capability_id, cluster_signature, status`) and `remediation_tier_transitions` (`project_id, cluster_signature, recorded_at`). Pinned both to their existing 63-char on-disk names in `backend/src/models/PreparedRemediationPlan.ts` and `RemediationTierTransition.ts`. All three truncated indexes in the DB are now covered; sweep confirms no others.
  - Verification: zero DB migration (names match on-disk). Post-deploy: prod backend boot logs show no `sync({ alter: true }) failed ... already exists` for any table. tsc unaffected (config-only).
  - Notes: Root-cause class is identifier-length. A durable guard would be a startup lint or a test asserting every model index name <= 63 chars; logged as a follow-up idea, not built here.

- [x] **server boot: gate sequelize.sync({ alter: true }) behind DB_BOOT_SYNC (default off) — fixes ~6-min boot.**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-r7m2
  - What changed: `backend/src/server.ts` ran `sequelize.sync({ alter: true })` (+ create-only fallback) on every boot. Schema is already managed by the explicit `ensure*Schema` functions (see the comment on `ensureOpsCommandCenterSchema` — sync is known-unreliable on this 215-model graph). Once the over-long index names were pinned, sync stopped failing fast on them and instead ran a full per-table introspection across all 215 models (~6 min on prod) before erroring on a pre-existing enum drift (`anthropic_content_registry.content_type`) — turning every boot/deploy into a multi-minute API outage. Wrapped the whole sync block in `if (process.env.DB_BOOT_SYNC === 'true')`, default off. Boot now skips it and relies on the explicit schema-ensure path.
  - Verification: post-deploy the prod backend binds in ~90s again (no full introspection), `Server running on port 3001`, site + API 200/401. Set `DB_BOOT_SYNC=true` only for a deliberate, supervised reconciliation.
  - Notes: This is the operational complement to the index-name pins — those made the logs clean; this keeps boot fast and removes the dependency on the unreliable sync. The enum drift (`anthropic_content_type` → `enum_anthropic_content_registry_content_type`) remains as a separate, deliberate migration if auto-sync is ever wanted.

- [x] **Gov-bid builds moved to per-build fork+PR repos; `createGovBidBuildLists` git instructions corrected.**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260616-q4m7
  - What changed: Interns kept hitting permission-denied pushing gov-bid build scaffolds at the Enterprise repo's `main` (Obi as `obikings04`; Ram earlier). Root cause: the build-task instructions said only "Commit + push. PR merged" with no branch/fork/repo guidance, and builds were modeled as `gov-bid-builds/<slug>/` subfolders of the Enterprise monorepo — so each intern's local Claude improvised a standalone Temp repo aimed at Enterprise `main` (which, if force-pushed, could have overwritten production). Fixed the model: each gov-bid build now lives in its OWN private repo under `ColaberryIntern` (`tdcj-oig-records`, `tdhca-multifamily`, `utd-residential-life`, `harris-agenda-meeting`); interns work in a fork and open PRs back; the `ColaberryIntern` account reviews/merges; Enterprise is untouchable. Each build repo carries a `CLAUDE.md` (git guardrails aimed at the intern's own Claude) + `INTERN_WORKFLOW.md` (exact fork→branch→PR flow). Enabled branch protection on Enterprise `main` (PR + 1 approval, no force-push, no deletion). Corrected `backend/src/scripts/createGovBidBuildLists.js`: paths are now build-repo-root-relative (`repoPath=''`), added `buildRepo`/`buildRepoUrl`, rewrote the "review scaffolded structure" task and the per-feature git step to fork→branch→PR with an explicit "do not self-merge," and tightened the done-when.
  - Verification: `node --check backend/src/scripts/createGovBidBuildLists.js` passes. All four build repos confirmed live, private, default branch `main` (`gh api repos/ColaberryIntern/<slug>`). Enterprise `main` protection confirmed via `gh api .../branches/main/protection` (required_pull_request_reviews present, allow_force_pushes:false, allow_deletions:false). Shipped via PR to `origin/main`.
  - Notes: Build-repo creation + branch protection are GitHub infra (performed as the shared `ColaberryIntern` account, which owns every repo), not Enterprise-repo code. The script change affects FUTURE BC build-list generation only; BC todolists already created are unchanged. Obi (`obikings04`) invited as read collaborator to `tdcj-oig-records` so he can fork; the other three interns' personal GitHub usernames are unknown (all prior `-workspace` commits were by the shared account) — invites for Akiwam/Omolola/samrawit pending those usernames from Ali. Shipped from an isolated git worktree (`origin/main` base) because the active branch held another session's uncommitted work.

- [x] **ops Basecamp client — in-container token auto-refresh from CCPP on 401 (stop OpsBcSync auth failures on token rotation).**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: The Basecamp token rotates ~every 2 weeks; the backend container reads `BASECAMP_ACCESS_TOKEN` from its baked `.env`, which goes stale on each rotation, so container-side BC calls (`bcSyncService`/OpsBcSync — the read-mirror feeding the priorityEngine — and `basecampClient`) 401'd until the `.env` was manually updated + redeployed. The host cron scripts already self-heal via `cron-env-wrapper.sh`; this adds the container-side equivalent. New `backend/src/services/ops/basecampToken.ts`: serves the env token normally and, on an auth failure, pulls the live token from `CCPP.Basecamp_AuthInfo` (single-flight, env fallback) and caches it. `basecampClient.ts` + `bcSyncService.ts` now resolve the token via the provider and **refresh-from-CCPP + retry once on a 401** (replacing their identical env-only `getBasecampToken()` + token-passing). (TS module, not the host scripts' CommonJS `basecampToken.js`, because the container runs from compiled `dist/`.)
  - Verification: `npx jest basecampToken.test.ts basecampClient.test.ts` → 13/13 (isAuthError; env resolve + Bearer strip; CCPP-absent fallback; single-flight; bcGet/bcPost retry-once-on-401, no-retry-on-200/500). `tsc --noEmit` clean (host build exit 0). Deployed to prod (`up -d --build backend`); **end-to-end proof in the running container**: `docker exec ... node -e refreshBcToken()` logged "refreshed token from CCPP after auth failure" and returned the live 394-char token, with 0 auth errors post-deploy.
  - Notes: Chosen over a refresh-cron guardrail (the other option) as the proper fix. Also did the immediate manual `.env` token update + restart earlier this session to stop the active 401s; this makes the next rotation self-heal without that. Shipped from an isolated worktree off `origin/main`.

- [x] **ops Basecamp client — rate-limit handling for OpsBcSync (429 backoff + proactive pacing + single-flight).**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: Once the token auto-refresh let OpsBcSync authenticate again, it surfaced that the sync 429-throttled hard (227-348 `429 API rate limit` errors/run) walking ~30k todos each cycle, so the ops mirror synced incompletely. New `backend/src/services/ops/bcRetry.ts`: `bcBackoffMs` (Retry-After-aware capped backoff), `BC_RETRYABLE_STATUS` (429/503), and `bcPace`/`paceDelayMs` (proactive ~250ms inter-call pacing, env `BC_MIN_INTERVAL_MS`, to stay under BC's ~50-req/10s limit so 429s rarely happen at all). Wired into `bcSyncService` + `basecampClient` `bcGet`/`bcPost` (retry-on-429 + pace-before-each-call). Added a **single-flight guard** to `runBcSync` (renamed body to `runBcSyncInner`): a paced pass can exceed the 2-min cron, so concurrent ticks now cleanly skip instead of stacking and fighting for the rate-limit budget.
  - Verification: `npx jest bcRetry.test.ts basecampToken.test.ts basecampClient.test.ts` → 21/21 (backoff math, pace math, 429 retry + retry-cap, single retry-on-401, no-retry on 200/500). `tsc --noEmit` clean. Deployed to prod; live: **0 `429 API rate limit` errors** (was 227-348/run), mirror refreshing completely (~16k of 30k rows / 12 min), single-flight skipping intervening ticks cleanly, backend healthy (cpu 13%, mem ~400MB).
  - Notes: Trade-off — a COMPLETE refresh of the ~30k-todo mirror now takes ~20 min because BC's ~5 req/s rate limit caps throughput (you cannot sync 30k items in 2 min); the old "2-min" sync was only fast because it dropped the 429'd calls (incomplete). Real freshness lever is reducing call volume — incremental/events-based sync or filtering to active/CB-managed projects (the file's own "Phase 1+" note) — which is r7m2's ops-mirror area, flagged separately. Pacing is env-tunable. [SUPERSEDED next entry: incremental sync implemented, ~20min -> ~3s.]

- [x] **OpsBcSync — incremental Todo-feed sync (replaces full per-project walk): ~20 min -> ~3 s.**
  - Date: 2026-06-16
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: A complete sync re-walked every project -> todoset -> todolist -> todo (~2500 BC calls for the ~30k-todo mirror), which under BC's ~5 req/s limit took ~20 min/pass — unacceptable. Replaced the walk in `bcSyncService.ts` with an **incremental sweep** of Basecamp's account-wide Todo recordings feed (`/projects/recordings.json?type=Todo&sort=updated_at&direction=desc`, paginated): each feed item carries the todo's fields + its project (`bucket`) + todolist (`parent`), so it upserts identically. New helpers `getSyncCursorMs` (high-water mark = `max(bc_updated_at)` minus a 10-min overlap) and `fetchUpdatedTodos` (sweep newest-first, stop at the first all-older page; `null` cursor backfills once, capped at FEED_MAX_PAGES). The project list is still fetched for `ops_bc_projects` + dormant-autodetect, but the per-project todo walk is gone. Same pattern that made the CB dispatcher 38x faster.
  - Verification: new `bcSyncIncremental.test.ts` → 4/4 (cursor stop, empty-page stop, null-cursor backfill, malformed-item skip); full ops BC suite 25/25; `tsc --noEmit` clean. **Live, direct-timed in the prod container: `runBcSync()` = 3.0–7.5 s** (was ~20 min), errors 0, seen 3 (only the todos changed since the cursor). Mirror intact at 29,997 rows; downstream priority + automation chain runs each cycle; 0 `429` errors.
  - Notes: Steady state touches only changed todos (seconds); a one-time backfill of an empty mirror still costs ~1 full sweep but that's rare and self-continues across runs as the cursor advances. Residual gaps from the pre-fix incomplete syncs self-heal as those todos next change (updated_at crosses the cursor). Combined with the rate-limit entry above (pacing + single-flight + 429 backoff), OpsBcSync is now fast, complete, and rate-safe.

- [x] **OpsBcSync — exclude Center of Excellence (student) projects; mirror 30k -> 4k.**
  - Date: 2026-06-17
  - Session: CC-20260615-h8k4
  - What changed: Per Ali, the "Center of Excellence" projects are student coursework with a huge todo volume that should NOT be tracked in the ops "waiting on human" queue — and they were ~25,958 of the ~30,000 mirror rows (~86%), i.e. the whole reason the sync was slow. `bcSyncService.ts`: new exported `isExcludedProject(name)` (matches `/center of excellence/i`, env-overridable via `OPS_SYNC_EXCLUDE_NAME_RE`), applied in the project loop (skip upsert) and the feed sweep (skip + don't count). `fetchUpdatedTodos` now also takes the real ops `bucketIds` and passes `&bucket=...` to the recordings feed so it's scoped server-side and never even fetches the high-churn CoE buckets. A one-time DB purge removed the existing CoE rows.
  - Verification: tests 9/9 (CoE name variants in/out, feed skips CoE without early-stop, `bucket=` param present/absent); `tsc --noEmit` clean. Live: the one-time purge took the mirror 30,000 -> 4,042 (deleted 25,958 todos + the CoE project rows); post-deploy a timed `runBcSync()` = ~2.4 s steady-state, 0 errors, CoE rows stay 0, mirror stable at ~4k.
  - Notes: Name-match auto-handles renamed/new CoE projects; add more exclusions via `OPS_SYNC_EXCLUDE_NAME_RE`. The purge was an operational one-off (direct DELETE, not a committed script). RMG Mortgage + other non-CoE projects were left in (Ali only excluded CoE).
